issues.description: Framing 'Watson and Crick figured out the structure of DNA. They learned how to READ the code of life' is loose. Solving the double-helix structure (1953) did not teach anyone to 'read' the code — that came later with sequencing (Sanger, 1977) and ultimately the genetic code work of Nirenberg/Khorana/Holley in the 1960s. Also, CRISPR is not 'writing' the code from scratch; it is targeted editing. Tighten the metaphor: '1953: we saw the structure. 2020: we could precisely edit it.'
Rosalind Franklin and Maurice Wilkins are omitted from the 1953 framing. Not strictly required for AP, but a PhD reviewer would flag the standard correction.

Says restriction enzymes 'evolved as a defense against viruses.' Accurate, but the narration should mention that bacteria protect their own DNA via methylation at the same recognition sites — this is a common AP free-response follow-up ('why doesn't the enzyme cut the bacterium's own genome?').

Definition of palindrome is slightly muddled: 'reads the same forward on one strand as backward on the other.' The technical AP-correct phrasing is that the 5'->3' sequence on one strand equals the 5'->3' sequence on the complementary strand. Current phrasing is okay but could mislead a careful student into thinking GAATTC == CTTAAG read backwards.

Calling a plasmid 'a small circular piece of bacterial DNA' is fine for AP, but missing that plasmids are extrachromosomal and replicate independently of the chromosome — a frequent AP MCQ distractor.

'30 cycles gets you to about a billion copies' — technically 2^30 ~ 1.07 x 10^9, so this is fine; but immediately after the script also says '10-to-the-ninth' in 08, which is the CED phrasing. Consistent, just flag that real PCR efficiency is <100%, so 'about a billion' is an idealized number. Not wrong, just an oversimplification.

Primers should be specified as short single-stranded DNA oligonucleotides that flank the target region (forward and reverse). 'Short primers bind to their complementary template regions' is technically true but a student who has not heard 'two primers, one on each strand' may not understand why the product is bounded.

'$3 billion in 2003 to under $1,000 today' is the standard Human Genome Project / NHGRI talking point and is accurate, but it is not in the CED excerpt. Flag for citation review (Reviewer C territory).

Calling CRISPR-Cas9 'the newest tool and the most powerful' is editorializing. Also, the script conflates two repair pathways without naming them: NHEJ (non-homologous end joining, which causes the indels) and HDR (homology-directed repair, used when a template is supplied). Naming them is not required by the CED, but a PhD would note the omission.

'CRISPR clinical trials are now treating sickle-cell disease' is true but understates current status — Casgevy (exa-cel) received FDA approval in December 2023, so it is now an approved therapy, not just a trial. Minor currency issue.

'recombinant insulin was the first FDA-approved biotech drug in 1982' — Humulin was approved by the FDA in October 1982. Accurate. No fix needed; flagged for cross-check by Reviewer C since the year is a specific claim.

Germline vs somatic framing is correct and useful. Could be sharpened: germline edits affect gametes / embryos / the germ line; somatic edits affect only differentiated (non-reproductive) cells of one individual. Currently says 'sperm or egg DNA' which is fine but missing the early-embryo case (where the famous He Jiankui CCR5 edits occurred).

summary: No outright factual errors; the molecular biology is accurate and AP-appropriate. The issues are oversimplifications and missing nuance (palindrome definition, methylation self-protection, NHEJ vs HDR, primer pairing, Casgevy approval status). Safe to ship after a light revision pass on sections 02, 04, 08, and 12.
